story_beats.title: The radiologist who automated her own morning
story_beats.body: Elena Moreau spent seven years reading chest films. In June she stood at MedHack Paris and let her model draft the findings on a live anonymized film in 40 seconds — the pre-read that used to eat half her morning. The room stood up.

facts.claim: MedHack Paris 2026 grand-prize winner

story_beats.title: Seven years in the reading room
story_beats.body: Elena practiced radiology at a Paris university hospital before going full-time on RadiAssist in March. Fun fact: her training dataset began with films she annotated herself on night shifts — which is why small-clinic workflows, not hospital PACS, are her wedge.

facts.claim: Hospital radiologist 2019–2026, per LinkedIn

signals.text: Five hardware board revisions in four months

evidence.claim: Public hardware iteration log

story_beats.title: The ring that listens for sepsis
story_beats.body: Sepsis kills quietly, often after the patient has gone home. Marco Ricci's smart ring watches temperature, HRV, and perfusion around the clock and raises the alarm hours earlier than a worried phone call. It nearly took the HackZurich crown — losing only to NeuroTriage.

facts.claim: HackZurich Health 2026 runner-up

story_beats.title: Built in a Politecnico lab, revised five times
story_beats.body: Marco and two labmates from Politecnico di Milano designed the board, firmware, and models themselves — five hardware revisions in four months, all logged publicly. Fun fact: revision three failed because a resin casing muffled the optical sensor; the log entry is titled 'lesson: skin, not plastic.'

facts.claim: 5 board revisions in 4 months, public log

story_beats.title: Scorecard: 7.0 — strong build, unproven demand
story_beats.body: Grit (7.6) and Pitch (7.2) are solid; Sell (6.2, low confidence) is the honest weak spot — no users or pilots yet, just stated interest from one surgical ward. The scorecard says what it can't verify rather than rounding up.

facts.claim: Sell dimension scored on a single unverified ward-interest mention

story_beats.title: A near-win and a public build log
story_beats.body: The runner-up finish at HackZurich in May put the project on the map; since then the signal is velocity, not traction — a steady public hardware log and firmware commits, with clinical validation still ahead.

facts.claim: Continuous firmware commits Apr–Jul 2026

project_description: A discrete-event simulation engine that models patient flow through hospital departments, letting administrators test staffing and bed-allocation changes in silico before committing to them.

scorecard.verify_offline_note: Two GitHub identities ('jkeller-eth' and 'jonaskdev') could not be conclusively merged, and the MedHack side-challenge win is attributed only to 'J. Keller'. Scores are computed on the merged view; verify identity before outreach.

signals.text: MedHack Paris 2026 side-challenge win attributed to 'J. Keller' (unverified link)

evidence.claim: Side-challenge winner listed as 'J. Keller, simulation tooling'

signals.text: Steady commits across two probable accounts since April

evidence.claim: Overlapping commit patterns on jkeller-eth and jonaskdev

story_beats.title: A hospital in silico — and an identity puzzle
story_beats.body: PulseGraph can rehearse a hospital's worst Monday before it happens: close a ward in the simulation, watch the queues ripple. The work is real and public. What Profound can't fully verify is whether the two GitHub accounts behind it are the same Jonas Keller — so this profile says so upfront.

facts.claim: Simulation benchmarked at 10k-bed scale

story_beats.title: The ETH simulation group, probably
story_beats.body: The primary account sits in ETH Zurich's simulation research group, with two labmates contributing. A second account, 'jonaskdev', shows matching commit rhythms and overlapping code style. Our entity-resolution model puts the match at 0.62 — likely, not certain.

facts.claim: Entity-resolution confidence 0.62 across two GitHub identities

story_beats.title: Scorecard: 6.2 — flagged 'verify offline'
story_beats.body: This is what an honest scorecard looks like when the footprint is thin: Sell is scored 5.5 on low confidence, one evidence item is explicitly marked inferred, and the whole card carries a verify-offline flag. A thin public trail is a research posture, not a disqualifier — but it is your diligence call, not ours.

facts.claim: Sell dimension rests on an inferred, unconfirmed hospital conversation

story_beats.title: A win that needs one phone call to confirm
story_beats.body: The MedHack Paris side-challenge lists 'J. Keller, simulation tooling' as a winner — almost certainly him, not provably him. Commit velocity across both accounts has been steady since April. One email to the MedHack organizers resolves the whole question.

facts.claim: Side-challenge win attributed to 'J. Keller'

story_beats.title: Fit: 55 / 100 — pending verification
story_beats.body: Hospital-operations simulation fits Meridian's thesis on paper, and research-grade depth is rare. The score is suppressed by uncertainty, not weakness: if identity and the hospital conversation both verify, our model re-rates him into the high 70s. That's the upside case for one phone call.

facts.claim: Fit assessment is a model opinion derived from your VC profile, not a sourced fact

story_beats.title: Verify first, then reach out
story_beats.body: Recommended sequence: confirm the MedHack attribution with the organizers, then reach the primary GitHub account's listed ETH address. Going in cold on the wrong identity burns the intro — this card exists so you don't.

facts.claim: ETH contact address listed on primary account profile
